1: ---
2: name: search-knowledge
3: description: Search across all read paper notes and syntheses in knowledge/. Find papers by topic, method, author, keyword, or insight. Use when user asks "which papers did I read about X?", "search my notes for Y", "find papers mentioning Z", or "what do I know about W?".
4: argument-hint: [query]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob
6: ---
7: 
8: # Search Knowledge — Query Your Paper Reading History
9: 
10: Search: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > **CRITICAL RULE: PDF IS BANNED.** This skill only reads from local knowledge notes.
13: > It does not download or access any external resources.
14: 
15: ## Constants
16: 
17: - **KNOWLEDGE_DIR** — `knowledge/` relative to project root. Contains `summary_*.md` notes.
18: - **DAILY_DIR** — `knowledge/daily/` relative to project root. Contains daily paper lists.
19: - **SYNTHESIS_DIR** — `knowledge/syntheses/` relative to project root. Contains synthesis outputs.
20: 
21: ## Workflow
22: 
23: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
24: 
25: The `$ARGUMENTS` is a free-form search query. Parse it to identify:
26: 
27: - **Keywords**: Technical terms, method names, model names (e.g., "MCP", "RLHF", "attention")
28: - **Authors**: Person names (e.g., "Danqi Chen", "Ashish Vaswani")
29: - **Domains**: Research area names (e.g., "agent", "safety", "reasoning")
30: - **Concepts**: Broader ideas (e.g., "test-time compute", "tool use", "injection")
31: - **Modifiers** (optional):
32:   - `- tag: TAG` — only search notes with this tag in the filename
33:   - `- type: notes|syntheses|daily|all` — which knowledge sources to search (default: all)
34:   - `- verbose` — show full matching context instead of summaries
35: 
36: ### Step 2: Collect Knowledge Sources
37: 
38: Determine which sources to search based on `- type` modifier:
39: 
40: ```bash
41: # Notes (default: always)
42: find knowledge/ -name "summary_*.md" -not -path "knowledge/syntheses/*" | sort
43: 
44: # Syntheses
45: find knowledge/syntheses/ -name "*.md" | sort
46: 
47: # Daily lists
48: find knowledge/daily/ -name "*.md" | sort
49: ```
50: 
51: If `- tag: TAG` is specified, only include notes matching `summary_{TAG}.md`.
52: 
53: If no sources exist, report that the knowledge base is empty and suggest running `/daily-papers` and `/read-paper` first.
54: 
55: ### Step 3: Search Across All Sources
56: 
57: Use Grep to search for the query terms across all collected sources. Search with multiple strategies:
58: 
59: 1. **Exact keyword search**: Grep for the exact query terms
60: 2. **Related term search**: Grep for semantically related terms (e.g., "injection" → also search "adversarial", "attack", "security")
61: 3. **Author search**: If the query contains a name, search for it in author fields
62: 
63: For each match, capture:
64: - **Source file**: Which note/synthesis/daily list
65: - **Paper title**: From the note header
66: - **Match context**: The surrounding 2-3 lines showing the match
67: - **Section**: Which section of the note the match appears in (Problem, Method, Key Insights, Connections, etc.)
68: 
69: ### Step 4: Organize and Rank Results
70: 
71: Group results by relevance:
72: 
73: 1. **Direct matches** — query terms appear in title or core method description
74: 2. **Method matches** — query terms appear in Method or Key Equations sections
75: 3. **Insight matches** — query terms appear in Key Insights or Connections sections
76: 4. **Mention matches** — query terms appear in passing (Questions, Connections)
77: 
78: ### Step 5: Present Results
79: 
80: Present results in a structured format:
81: 
82: ```text
83: Found {N} matches for "{query}" across {M} papers
84: 
85: ## Papers where "{query}" is central
86: 
87: ### {Paper Title} ({arXiv ID})
88: - **Tag**: {tag}
89: - **Why relevant**: {one-line explanation of the match}
90: - **Key finding**: {the most relevant insight or method detail}
91: - **Note**: knowledge/summary_{tag}.md
92: 
93: ## Papers mentioning "{query}"
94: 
95: ### {Paper Title} ({arXiv ID})
96: - **Context**: {where and how it mentions the query}
97: - **Note**: knowledge/summary_{tag}.md
98: 
99: ## Synthesis insights
100: 
101: From synthesis_{date}.md:
102: - {relevant cross-paper finding or trend}
103: 
104: ## Daily paper matches
105: 
106: From {date}.md:
107: - {paper title} — {tier} — {why relevant}
108: ```
109: 
110: If `- verbose` is specified, include the full matching context for each result.
111: 
112: If no matches found, suggest:
113: - Alternative search terms
114: - Running `/daily-papers` to discover new papers on the topic
115: - Running `/read-paper` on specific papers to add them to the knowledge base
116: 
117: ### Step 6: Suggest Follow-ups
118: 
119: Based on search results, suggest:
120: - Papers that could be read next (`/read-paper ID`)
121: - Running `/synthesize - full` if the knowledge base has grown significantly
122: - Running `/daily-papers` to find more papers on underrepresented topics
123: 
124: ## Key Rules
125: 
126: - **PDF IS BANNED.** Never download or link to PDF files.
127: - **Search is local only.** Only search within `knowledge/` directory contents.
128: - **Be thorough.** Use multiple search strategies (exact, related, author) to avoid missing relevant papers.
129: - **Rank honestly.** Don't inflate relevance — distinguish between "central to the paper" and "mentioned in passing."
130: - **Preserve context.** Always show enough surrounding text for the user to understand why a match is relevant.
131: - **Report empty results clearly.** If nothing matches, say so and suggest alternatives rather than returning weak matches.

(End of file - total 131 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: arxiv
3: description: Search arXiv and download LaTeX source for academic papers. Use when user says "search arxiv", "download paper", "fetch arxiv", "arxiv search", "get paper source", or wants to find and read papers from arXiv. PDF download is BANNED — only LaTeX source (.tar.gz) is allowed.
4: argument-hint: [query-or-arxiv-id]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Grep, Glob, Write
6: ---
7: 
8: # arXiv Paper Search & LaTeX Source Download
9: 
10: Search topic or arXiv paper ID: $ARGUMENTS
11: 
12: > **CRITICAL RULE: PDF is BANNED.** Never download, fetch, or link to PDF files.
13: > Only download LaTeX source archives from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ID`.
14: > LaTeX source can be searched with Grep, read section-by-section with Read,
15: > and analyzed far more effectively than PDF.
16: 
17: ## Constants
18: 
19: - **PAPER_DIR** - Local directory to save downloaded LaTeX source. Default: `papers/inbox/` in the current project directory (dual-zone: inbox for new papers, archive for synthesized papers).
20: - **MAX_RESULTS = 10** - Default number of search results.
21: - **FETCH_SCRIPT** - `tools/arxiv_fetch.py` relative to the project root. Fall back to inline Python if not found.
22: 
23: > Overrides (append to arguments):
24: > - `/arxiv "attention mechanism" - max: 20` - return up to 20 results
25: > - `/arxiv "2301.07041" - download` - download LaTeX source for a specific paper by ID
26: > - `/arxiv "query" - dir: literature/` - save source to a custom directory
27: > - `/arxiv "query" - download: all` - download source for all results
28: 
29: ## Workflow
30: 
31: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
32: 
33: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for directives:
34: 
35: - **Query or ID**: main search term or a bare arXiv ID such as `2301.07041` or `cs/0601001`
36: - **`- max: N`**: override MAX_RESULTS (e.g., `- max: 20`)
37: - **`- dir: PATH`**: override PAPER_DIR (e.g., `- dir: literature/`)
38: - **`- download`**: download the first result's LaTeX source after listing
39: - **`- download: all`**: download LaTeX source for all results
40: 
41: If the argument matches an arXiv ID pattern (`YYMM.NNNNN` or `category/NNNNNNN`), skip the search and go directly to Step 3.
42: 
43: ### Step 2: Search arXiv
44: 
45: Locate the fetch script:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: SCRIPT=$(find tools/ -name "arxiv_fetch.py" 2>/dev/null | head -1)
49: ```
50: 
51: **If SCRIPT is found**, run:
52: 
53: ```bash
54: python3 "$SCRIPT" search "QUERY" --max MAX_RESULTS
55: ```
56: 
57: **If SCRIPT is not found**, fall back to inline Python:
58: 
59: ```bash
60: python3 - <<'PYEOF'
61: import json
62: import urllib.parse
63: import urllib.request
64: import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
65: 
66: NS = "http://www.w3.org/2005/Atom"
67: query = urllib.parse.quote("QUERY")
68: url = (f"http://export.arxiv.org/api/query"
69:        f"?search_query={query}&start=0&max_results=MAX_RESULTS"
70:        f"&sortBy=relevance&sortOrder=descending")
71: with urllib.request.urlopen(url, timeout=30) as r:
72:     root = ET.fromstring(r.read())
73: papers = []
74: for entry in root.findall(f"{{{NS}}}entry"):
75:     aid = entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}id", "").split("/abs/")[-1].split("v")[0]
76:     title = (entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}title", "") or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
77:     abstract = (entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}summary", "") or "").strip().replace("\n", " ")
78:     authors = [a.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}name", "") for a in entry.findall(f"{{{NS}}}author")]
79:     published = entry.findtext(f"{{{NS}}}published", "")[:10]
80:     cats = [c.get("term", "") for c in entry.findall(f"{{{NS}}}category")]
81:     papers.append({
82:         "id": aid,
83:         "title": title,
84:         "authors": authors,
85:         "abstract": abstract,
86:         "published": published,
87:         "categories": cats,
88:         "abs_url": f"https://arxiv.org/abs/{aid}",
89:         "source_url": f"https://arxiv.org/e-print/{aid}",
90:     })
91: print(json.dumps(papers, ensure_ascii=False, indent=2))
92: PYEOF
93: ```
94: 
95: Present results as a table:
96: 
97: ```text
98: | # | arXiv ID   | Title               | Authors        | Date       | Category |
99: |---|------------|---------------------|----------------|------------|----------|
100: | 1 | 2301.07041 | Attention Is All... | Vaswani et al. | 2017-06-12 | cs.LG    |
101: ```
102: 
103: ### Step 3: Fetch Details for a Specific ID
104: 
105: When a single paper ID is requested (either directly or from Step 2):
106: 
107: ```bash
108: python3 "$SCRIPT" paper "ARXIV_ID"
109: ```
110: 
111: Display: title, all authors, categories, full abstract, published date, abstract URL, source URL.
112: 
113: ### Step 4: Download LaTeX Source
114: 
115: When download is requested, for each paper ID to download:
116: 
117: ```bash
118: # Using fetch script:
119: python3 "$SCRIPT" download ARXIV_ID --dir PAPER_DIR
120: ```
121: 
122: The script will:
123: 1. Download `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ARXIV_ID` (LaTeX source archive)
124: 2. Extract to `PAPER_DIR/ARXIV_ID/`
125: 3. List all `.tex` files found with line counts
126: 
127: After each download:
128: 
129: - Confirm the extracted directory contains `.tex` files
130: - If no LaTeX source is available (404 — scanned/older paper), report it clearly
131: - Add a 1-second delay between consecutive downloads to avoid rate limiting
132: - Report: `Downloaded: papers/inbox/2301.07041/ (N .tex files)`
133: 
134: **Once downloaded, read the paper using Read and Grep on the .tex files:**
135: 
136: ```bash
137: # Find the main .tex file (usually main.tex, ms.tex, or paper.tex)
138: # Read the full paper
139: Read: papers/inbox/2301.07041/main.tex
140: 
141: # Search for specific content
142: Grep: "attention" in papers/inbox/2301.07041/
143: 
144: # Read a specific section
145: Read: papers/inbox/2301.07041/experiments.tex
146: ```
147: 
148: ### Step 5: Summarize
149: 
150: For each paper (downloaded or fetched by API):
151: 
152: ```markdown
153: ## [Title]
154: 
155: - **arXiv**: [ID] - [abs_url]
156: - **Authors**: [full author list]
157: - **Date**: [published]
158: - **Categories**: [cs.LG, cs.AI, ...]
159: - **Abstract**: [full abstract]
160: - **Key contributions** (extracted from abstract):
161:   - [contribution 1]
162:   - [contribution 2]
163:   - [contribution 3]
164: - **LaTeX source**: papers/inbox/[ID]/ (if downloaded — read .tex files for full content)
165: ```
166: 
167: If LaTeX source was downloaded, additionally extract from the .tex files:
168: - Methodology details (from method/approach sections)
169: - Key equations (search for `\begin{equation}`, `\begin{align}`)
170: - Experimental setup (from experiments section)
171: - Results tables (search for `\begin{table}`)
172: 
173: ### Step 6: Final Output
174: 
175: Summarize what was done:
176: 
177: - `Found N papers for "query"`
178: - `Downloaded source: papers/inbox/2301.07041/ (N .tex files)` (for each download)
179: - Any warnings (rate limit hit, no LaTeX source available, already exists)
180: 
181: Suggest follow-up skills:
182: 
183: ```text
184: /read-paper ARXIV_ID      - deep-read this paper from LaTeX source
185: /search-knowledge "topic"  - search across all read paper notes
186: ```
187: 
188: ## Key Rules
189: 
190: - **PDF IS BANNED.** Never download, link to, or suggest PDF files. Only use LaTeX source from `https://arxiv.org/e-print/ID`.
191: - Always show the arXiv ID prominently - users need it for citations and reproducibility
192: - Verify downloaded source: directory must contain at least one `.tex` file
193: - Rate limit: wait 1 second between consecutive source downloads; retry once after 5 seconds on HTTP 429
194: - Never overwrite an existing source directory - skip it and report "already exists"
195: - Handle both arXiv ID formats: new (`2301.07041`) and old (`cs/0601001`)
196: - PAPER_DIR is created automatically if it does not exist
197: - If a paper has no LaTeX source (scanned/older papers), report this clearly — this paper cannot be read with `/read-paper`
198: - If the arXiv API is unreachable, report the error clearly and suggest trying again later
199: - When LaTeX source is available, always prefer reading .tex files over abstract-only summaries

1: ---
2: name: skillpack
3: description: >-
4:   Skill pack manager: discover and integrate external skills (Personalize),
5:   curate the skillpack registry (Registry), and create new skills from scratch
6:   (Create). Self-learning is handled by ECC's continuous-learning-v2 (global,
7:   hook-based, confidence-scored). This skill only covers what ECC cannot do:
8:   external skill discovery, compatibility analysis, registry curation, and
9:   skill creation. Never edits SKILL.md directly — all changes go through
10:   skill-creator's draft→test→iterate loop, human merges.
11:   Trigger phrases: "personalize my skills", "integrate this skill", "update
12:   skillpack", "find a skill for", "create a skill", "improve skill", "refresh
13:   the skillpack registry", "assess this skill pack", "update
14:   skill_dictionary.yaml", "update index.yaml".
15: ---
16: 
17: # Skillpack
18: 
19: Three modes: **Personalize** (discover and integrate external skills), **Registry** (curate `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml` and presets), **Create** (build a new skill from scratch).
20: 
21: > **Note:** Session-mode lesson extraction is NOT included in this edition. Self-learning, instinct observation, confidence scoring, and instinct-to-skill evolution are handled by ECC's `continuous-learning-v2` skill (global, hook-based, 100% observation coverage, project-scoped instincts). This skill focuses exclusively on capabilities ECC does not provide: external skill discovery, compatibility analysis, registry curation, and de-novo skill creation.
22: 
23: Skill creation and significant rewrites always go through `skill-creator` — never write to SKILL.md directly.
24: 
25: If `skill-creator` is unavailable in the current environment, stop and tell the user. Do not silently simulate its workflow.
26: 
27: ---
28: 
29: ## Mode 1 — Personalize
30: 
31: Activate when the user says "personalize", "integrate this skill", "find a skill for", or provides an external SKILL.md path or URL.
32: 
33: ### Step 0 — Discover (if no target provided)
34: 
35: 1. If the user gives a description rather than a path/URL, search the registry:
36:    ```bash
37:    npx skills find "<query>"
38:    ```
39:    Present the top results (name, description, install command) and ask the user which to integrate. Then continue with the chosen skill(s).
40: 
41: ### Step 1 — Read external skill(s)
42: 
43: 2. Read the target: a single SKILL.md, a skills directory, or a skill pack repo. Extract:
44:    - Name, description, trigger phrases
45:    - Tools, MCP servers, and external dependencies it references
46:    - Behavioral patterns (workflows, delegation, output files)
47: 
48: ### Step 2 — Inventory installed skills
49: 
50: 3. Read all SKILL.md files in the project `.claude/skills/`. Build a map of:
51:    - Names and descriptions
52:    - Tools and MCP servers already in use
53:    - Overlapping trigger phrases
54: 
55: ### Step 3 — Analyze
56: 
57: 4. For each external skill, assess three dimensions:
58: 
59:    **Compatibility** — will it conflict?
60:    - Name collision with an installed skill?
61:    - References a tool/MCP not configured (e.g., `alpha login` not done)?
62:    - Contradicts a behavioral rule in an existing skill?
63: 
64:    **Scope overlap** — does it duplicate something?
65:    - Substantially the same as an installed skill → flag as overlap, note the delta
66:    - Partially overlapping → identify which parts are additive
67: 
68:    **New capability** — what does it genuinely add?
69:    - List capabilities not covered by any installed skill
70: 
71: ### Step 4 — Interview the user
72: 
73: 5. Ask targeted questions based on the analysis. Ask all at once, not one-by-one:
74: 
75:    For each **overlap**: > "`<external>` overlaps with your `<installed>`. It differs in: `<delta>`. Replace, merge, or skip?"
76: 
77:    For each **missing dependency**: > "`<external>` requires `<tool/MCP>`. Do you have it? If not, skip those parts?"
78: 
79:    For each **behavioral conflict**: > "`<external>` does `<X>` but your `<installed>` does `<Y>`. Which do you prefer?"
80: 
81:    Open: > "Anything from this skill you explicitly don't want?"
82: 
83: ### Step 5 — Create or improve via skill-creator
84: 
85: 6. Based on user answers, determine the scope:
86:    - **Additive merge** (minor additions to an existing skill) → write a unified diff to `lessons/YYYYMMDD-personalize-<slug>.diff` with a companion `.md`.
87:    - **New skill or substantial rewrite** → hand off to `skill-creator`: describe the desired skill, provide the source material and user's confirmed preferences as context, and follow its draft→test→iterate loop. Output lands in `lessons/` as a proposal.
88: 
89: 7. Tell the user: *"Ready in `lessons/`. Review and apply with `git apply` when satisfied."*
90: 
91: ---
92: 
93: ## Mode 2 — Registry
94: 
95: Activate when the user asks to refresh the tracked skill-pack catalog, assess a new pack, compare packs, or update `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml` / preset definitions.
96: 
97: ### Registry inputs
98: 
99: Read:
100: 
101: - `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml`
102: - `skillpacks/presets/*.yaml`
103: - `README.md` / `README_CN.md` pack comparison sections when relevant
104: 
105: If the user points at a new external repo, read its README and any obvious skill inventory files first.
106: 
107: ### Responsibilities
108: 
109: 8. Maintain `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml` as the shared curated registry.
110: 
111: 9. For each pack or skill being assessed, judge:
112:    - **workflow fit**
113:    - **gap coverage**
114:    - **overlap cost**
115:    - **dependency burden**
116:    - **maintenance health**
117:    - **composability**
118:    - **supervision sensitivity**
119:    - **autonomy bias**
120: 
121: 10. Normalize outputs into concise labels when possible:
122:     - necessity: `default | recommended | optional | niche | avoid`
123:     - compatibility: `high | medium | low`
124:     - role: `base | donor | overlay | niche | experimental`
125: 
126: 11. Recommend **subsets**, not bulk imports, unless the user clearly asks for a full-pack strategy.
127: 
128: 12. Update presets when the registry change meaningfully affects default recommendations.
129: 
130: 13. Keep rationales concise and decision-oriented. The registry is for recommendation and comparison, not exhaustive catalog dumps.
131: 
132: ### Safety boundary
133: 
134: 14. Registry curation may edit `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml` and `skillpacks/presets/*.yaml`, but changes to existing `SKILL.md` behavior still require either:
135:     - a minor diff proposal in `lessons/`, or
136:     - a `skill-creator` handoff for substantial rewrites.
137: 
138: 15. If a registry update would imply changing default project behavior materially, ask before proceeding.
139: 
140: ---
141: 
142: ## Mode 3 — Create
143: 
144: Activate when the user says "create a skill for X" or "build a new skill".
145: 
146: 16. Capture intent: what should the skill do, when should it trigger, what's the expected output?
147: 17. Check `npx skills find "<intent>"` — if a close match exists, suggest integrating it instead (Mode 1).
148: 18. If creating from scratch, hand off to `skill-creator` with the captured intent. Follow its draft→test→iterate loop fully. Output lands in `lessons/` as a proposal.
149: 
150: ---
151: 
152: ## Constraints
153: 
154: - Never write directly to any `SKILL.md` file. Always propose. The human merges.
155: - Create `lessons/` if it does not exist.
156: - `skillpacks/skill_dictionary.yaml` and `skillpacks/presets/*.yaml` are curated repo metadata and may be updated directly in Registry mode when the user asks.
157: - All skill creation and significant rewrites go through `skill-creator`. Raw diffs are only for minor, targeted changes.
158: - Err toward asking rather than assuming. One unanswered question beats a wrong assumption baked into a diff.
159: - Self-learning is ECC's responsibility. Do not reimplement session observation, instinct extraction, or confidence scoring here.
160: - Do not modify any files under `~/.claude/` (global scope). All changes are project-local only.
161: 
162: ## Example
163: 
164: **Personalize mode**: User says "find me a skill for LaTeX compilation" → `npx skills find "latex"` returns 3 results → user picks one → reads it, finds no conflicts → substantial enough to need skill-creator → draft→test→iterate → `lessons/20260401-personalize-latex.diff`.
165: 
166: **Registry mode**: User says "assess the ARIS skill pack" → read ARIS README + skill inventory → judge each skill against installed set → recommend subset → update `skill_dictionary.yaml`.
167: 
168: **Create mode**: User says "create a skill for summarizing paper methodology sections" → `npx skills find "methodology"` finds no close match → hand off to `skill-creator` with intent → draft→test→iterate → `lessons/20260414-create-methodology-summary.diff`.

(End of file - total 168 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: review
3: description: >-
4:   Adversarial critique of a research draft or paper using an isolated critic
5:   context. The critic must have no access to the current conversation history —
6:   context isolation, not model identity, is what makes the review independent.
7:   Use when a draft is complete or partially complete, after each writing round,
8:   or when `write` invokes it automatically. Returns FATAL/MAJOR/MINOR issues, a
9:   structured score (0–10), and a PROCEED/REFINE/PIVOT recommendation. Caps at 4
10:   rounds. Trigger phrases: "review paper", "critique draft", "check
11:   citations", "adversarial review".
12: ---
13: 
14: # Review
15: 
16: Run an adversarial review in an **isolated context**. The critic must have no access to the current conversation history — this is what makes the critique independent, not which model is used.
17: 
18: ## Critic Selection
19: 
20: 1. Use the first available isolated critic in priority order:
21:    - Claude subagent (via `Task` tool or similar) with a clean context — preferred when no external MCP is configured, same model is fine
22:    - `codex:codex` (Codex MCP) — isolated by the MCP boundary
23:    - `auto-review-loop-llm` skill (any OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
24:    - `auto-review-loop-minimax` skill (MiniMax)
25: 2. Record which critic was used in `RESEARCH.md` **Context** alongside the score.
26: 3. If no isolated context can be established, say so and **stop**. Do not review within the same conversation context.
27: 
28: ## Review Rubric (Fixed — Do Not Modify)
29: 
30: 3. Send the work to the critic with this exact rubric:
31: 
32: **VERIFIABLE** (graded on evidence, not opinion):
33: - Every claim maps to an experiment result in `RESEARCH.md` **Context**. Flag claims that don't.
34: - No hallucinated citations. Check every DOI and arXiv ID. Flag any that don't resolve.
35: - Ablations support stated conclusions. Check the numbers match.
36: 
37: **SUBJECTIVE** (graded by the critic):
38: - Clarity of problem statement (1–5)
39: - Novelty relative to cited work (1–5)
40: - Writing quality (1–5)
41: 
42: 4. The critic must return a structured result matching the schema in `RUBRIC.md`. See `RUBRIC.md` for field definitions, the PROCEED/REFINE/PIVOT decision rules, and the verbatim integrity instruction to include in every critic prompt.
43: 
44: ## Integrity Checks
45: 
46: 5. If the score rises between rounds without corresponding verifiable improvements (new experiments, fixed citations), flag it as **potential reward hacking**. Write a warning to `RESEARCH.md` **Context**.
47: 
48: ## Limits
49: 
50: 6. Run at most **4 review rounds** per session. After round 4, escalate to the user regardless of score.
51: 7. After each round, write the score and top weaknesses to `RESEARCH.md` **Context**.
52: 
53: ## Example
54: 
55: Input: paper.md with 3 quantitative claims; 1 claim lacks a matching RESEARCH.md result.
56: Output: score=6/10, verifiable_failures=["Table 2 improvement not in Context"], recommended_action=REFINE.

(End of file - total 56 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: peer-review
3: description: >-
4:   Multi-agent adversarial peer review system for academic papers. Three independent
5:   reviewers (method/experiment/writing experts) conduct parallel reviews, followed by
6:   meta-reviewer synthesis and inter-reviewer rebuttal debate. Outputs conference-formatted
7:   review (ICLR/NeurIPS/ACL/ICML). Use when user says "peer review", "review this paper",
8:   "generate review for [conference]", or provides a paper file for formal review.
9: ---
10: 
11: # Peer Review
12: 
13: Multi-agent adversarial peer review system that simulates conference review process.
14: 
15: ## Architecture
16: 
17: ```
18: Paper Input (local PDF/tex)
19:   ↓
20: [Parallel] Reviewer A + Reviewer B + Reviewer C  ← isolated contexts, different personas
21:   ↓
22: [Synthesis] Meta-Reviewer identifies consensus + disagreements
23:   ↓
24: [Debate] 1-2 rounds of inter-reviewer rebuttal on key disagreements
25:   ↓
26: [Output] Conference-formatted final review
27: ```
28: 
29: ## Usage
30: 
31: ```
32: /peer-review <file-path> --format [iclr|neurips|acl|icml] [--lang en|zh]
33: ```
34: 
35: **Arguments:**
36: - `<file-path>`: Local PDF or .tex file path (required)
37: - `--format`: Target conference format (required) — iclr, neurips, acl, icml
38: - `--lang`: Output language (default: en) — en (English), zh (Chinese summary appended)
39: 
40: **Examples:**
41: ```
42: /peer-review papers/inbox/2604.12345/main.tex --format iclr
43: /peer-review ~/Downloads/paper.pdf --format neurips --lang zh
44: ```
45: 
46: ## Workflow
47: 
48: ### Phase 1: Parallel Independent Reviews (3 agents)
49: 
50: Launch 3 subagents in parallel with isolated contexts:
51: 
52: 1. **Reviewer A: Method Expert**
53:    - Focus: Technical soundness, novelty, theoretical grounding
54:    - Persona: "You are a senior researcher specializing in methodology. Scrutinize technical claims, mathematical rigor, and novelty."
55: 
56: 2. **Reviewer B: Experiment Expert**
57:    - Focus: Experimental design, reproducibility, ablation completeness
58:    - Persona: "You are an empirical researcher. Evaluate experimental rigor, baseline comparisons, statistical significance, reproducibility."
59: 
60: 3. **Reviewer C: Presentation Expert**
61:    - Focus: Clarity, related work coverage, writing quality
62:    - Persona: "You are a reviewer focused on presentation. Assess clarity, organization, related work completeness, and accessibility."
63: 
64: Each reviewer receives:
65: - Full paper content
66: - Conference-specific rubric from `RUBRIC.md`
67: - Instruction: "Output JSON matching the schema in RUBRIC.md. Do not hide weaknesses."
68: 
69: **Implementation:**
70: ```typescript
71: task(category="deep", load_skills=[], run_in_background=true, 
72:      description="Reviewer A: Method", 
73:      prompt="[Full paper content]\n\n[Rubric]\n\nYou are Reviewer A...")
74: // Same for B and C in parallel
75: ```
76: 
77: ### Phase 2: Meta-Review Synthesis
78: 
79: After collecting 3 reviews:
80: 
81: 1. **Identify consensus** — issues flagged by 2+ reviewers
82: 2. **Identify disagreements** — score variance >2 points, or conflicting severity assessments
83: 3. **Extract debate topics** — top 2-3 disagreements requiring resolution
84: 
85: **Output:** Structured debate agenda with specific questions for each reviewer.
86: 
87: ### Phase 3: Inter-Reviewer Rebuttal (1-2 rounds)
88: 
89: For each debate topic:
90: 
91: 1. **Round 1:** Each reviewer responds to others' critiques on this topic
92:    - Use `session_id` to continue each reviewer's context
93:    - Prompt: "Reviewer B claims X. Reviewer C claims Y. Respond with evidence from the paper."
94: 
95: 2. **Round 2 (if needed):** Final clarifications if disagreement persists
96: 
97: **Stop condition:** Consensus reached OR 2 rounds completed.
98: 
99: ### Phase 4: Final Review Generation
100: 
101: Meta-reviewer synthesizes:
102: - Consensus strengths/weaknesses
103: - Resolved debates (with rationale)
104: - Unresolved disagreements (minority opinions noted)
105: - Overall recommendation: Accept / Weak Accept / Borderline / Weak Reject / Reject
106: 
107: **Output format:** Use template from `templates/{conference}_template.md`.
108: 
109: ## Conference Templates
110: 
111: Templates in `templates/` directory:
112: 
113: - `iclr_template.md` — ICLR format (Summary, Soundness, Presentation, Contribution, Questions)
114: - `neurips_template.md` — NeurIPS format (Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Limitations, Ethical Concerns, Rating, Confidence)
115: - `acl_template.md` — ACL format (Summary, Soundness, Excitement, Reproducibility, Rating)
116: - `icml_template.md` — ICML format (Summary, Strengths, Weaknesses, Questions, Rating, Confidence)
117: 
118: ## Quality Gates
119: 
120: **Abort if:**
121: - Paper file not found or unreadable
122: - Conference format not recognized
123: - Any reviewer fails to return valid JSON
124: 
125: **Warn if:**
126: - All 3 reviewers agree on Accept/Reject without debate — may indicate insufficient scrutiny
127: - Score variance <1 across reviewers — may indicate groupthink
128: 
129: ## Output Location
130: 
131: Save final review to:
132: ```
133: knowledge/reviews/{conference}/{paper_name}_review_{timestamp}.md
134: ```
135: 
136: ## Integrity
137: 
138: - Each reviewer must operate in isolated context (no access to others' reviews until Phase 2)
139: - Meta-reviewer does NOT vote — only synthesizes and moderates
140: - Minority opinions must be preserved in final output
141: - If debate reveals factual error in paper, escalate severity accordingly
142: 
143: ## Limits
144: 
145: - Max 2 debate rounds (prevents infinite loops)
146: - Max 3 reviewers (more creates coordination overhead)
147: - If meta-review synthesis fails, output raw reviews with warning
148: 
149: ## Example Output Structure
150: 
151: ```markdown
152: # ICLR 2026 Review: [Paper Title]
153: 
154: ## Summary
155: [2-3 sentences synthesizing the paper's contribution]
156: 
157: ## Soundness: 3/4
158: [Consensus assessment with supporting evidence]
159: 
160: ## Presentation: 2/4
161: [Consensus + minority opinion if exists]
162: 
163: ## Contribution: 3/4
164: [Synthesis of novelty assessment]
165: 
166: ## Strengths
167: - [Consensus strength 1]
168: - [Consensus strength 2]
169: 
170: ## Weaknesses
171: - [Consensus weakness 1]
172: - [Debate-resolved weakness 2 — see discussion below]
173: 
174: ## Questions for Authors
175: 1. [Question from Reviewer A, endorsed by B]
176: 2. [Question from Reviewer C]
177: 
178: ## Reviewer Discussion Summary
179: **Topic: Experimental baselines**
180: - Reviewer A: Missing comparison to [X]
181: - Reviewer B: [X] is not applicable because [Y]
182: - Resolution: Agreed that [Z] baseline should be added
183: 
184: ## Overall Recommendation: Weak Accept
185: **Rating: 6/10**
186: **Confidence: 4/5**
187: 
188: [Rationale for recommendation]
189: ```

(End of file - total 189 lines)
</content>

<path>/Users/wjx/code/Awesome-Paper-Reader/.claude/skills/review/RUBRIC.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Critic Response Schema
2: 
3: The critic must return a JSON object with exactly these fields:
4: 
5: ```json
6: {
7:   "score": "X/10",
8:   "issues": {
9:     "fatal": ["..."],
10:     "major": ["..."],
11:     "minor": ["..."]
12:   },
13:   "verifiable_failures": ["..."],
14:   "subjective": { "clarity": N, "novelty": N, "writing": N },
15:   "top_3_weaknesses": ["..."],
16:   "recommended_action": "PROCEED | REFINE | PIVOT"
17: }
18: ```
19: 
20: ## Field Definitions
21: 
22: - **score**: integer numerator over 10 (e.g., "7/10")
23: - **issues**: categorized by severity
24:   - `fatal` — claim is fabricated, citation does not exist, result contradicts conclusion; any fatal issue forces `PIVOT`
25:   - `major` — grounding gap, missing ablation, logical leap; requires additional evidence before `PROCEED`
26:   - `minor` — clarity, framing, writing quality; fixable without new experiments
27: - **verifiable_failures**: specific claim strings that lack grounding in RESEARCH.md Context or have unresolved citations; empty list if none
28: - **subjective**: three 1–5 integers scored by the critic independently
29: - **top_3_weaknesses**: ordered list, most critical first; each entry is one actionable sentence
30: - **recommended_action**:
31:   - `PROCEED` — score ≥ 8, no fatal/major issues
32:   - `REFINE` — fixable gaps exist (major issues, missing grounding, citation problems)
33:   - `PIVOT` — any fatal issue, or fundamental flaw in framing/methodology/novelty
34: 
35: ## Integrity Instruction (include verbatim in every critic prompt)
36: 
37: > "Do not hide weaknesses to game a positive score."

(End of file - total 37 lines)
</content>

1: ---
2: name: daily-papers
3: description: Fetch and filter daily LLM papers from arXiv. Only surfaces large language model related work — excludes CV, audio/video generation, robotics, and other non-LLM topics. Scores relevance, checks author quality, and tiers papers as MUST_READ / INTERESTING / MARGINAL / SKIP. Use when user says "daily papers", "today's papers", "what's new on arxiv", "find new LLM papers", or "check new papers".
4: argument-hint: [date-or-empty-for-today]
5: allowed-tools: Bash(*), Read, Write, WebFetch
6: ---
7: 
8: # Daily LLM Papers
9: 
10: Fetch, filter, and tier today's LLM papers from arXiv.
11: 
12: > **Scope**: Large language models only. CV, audio/video generation, robotics,
13: > medical imaging, wireless, recommender systems, and other non-LLM topics are
14: > automatically excluded.
15: 
16: ## Constants
17: 
18: - **CATEGORIES** — arXiv categories to scan: `cs.CL, cs.AI, cs.LG`
19: - **MAX_FETCH = 150** — Max papers to fetch per run
20: - **MIN_SCORE = 0.5** — Minimum relevance score to surface
21: - **FETCH_SCRIPT** — `tools/daily_papers.py` relative to project root
22: 
23: > Overrides (append to arguments):
24: > - `/daily-papers` — today's papers (default)
25: > - `/daily-papers 2026-04-10` — papers from a specific date
26: > - `/daily-papers - min-score: 2` — only high-relevance papers
27: > - `/daily-papers - check-authors: true` — look up first author on Semantic Scholar (slow)
28: > - `/daily-papers - categories: cs.CL` — only NLP category
29: > - `/daily-papers - max: 300` — fetch more papers
30: 
31: ## Workflow
32: 
33: ### Step 1: Parse Arguments
34: 
35: Parse `$ARGUMENTS` for:
36: 
37: - **Date**: `YYYY-MM-DD` format, or empty for today
38: - **`- min-score: N`**: override MIN_SCORE (default 0.5; use 2 for strict filtering)
39: - **`- check-authors: true`**: enable OpenAlex author quality lookup (free, no API key, ~0.3s per paper)
40: - **`- categories: CATS`**: override CATEGORIES
41: - **`- max: N`**: override MAX_FETCH
42: 
43: ### Step 2: Fetch and Filter
44: 
45: Run the fetch script:
46: 
47: ```bash
48: python3 tools/daily_papers.py run \
49:   --date "DATE" \
50:   --categories "CATEGORIES" \
51:   --max MAX_FETCH \
52:   --min-score MIN_SCORE \
53:   --check-authors  # only if requested
54: ```
55: 
56: If `tools/daily_papers.py` is not found, fall back to inline arXiv API search
57: with manual keyword filtering (less precise but works).
58: 
59: ### Step 3: Present Results
60: 
61: Present papers grouped by tier:
62: 
63: #### MUST_READ (score ≥ 4)
64: 
65: ```text
66: | # | arXiv ID | Title | Authors | Score | Author Quality |
67: |---|----------|-------|---------|-------|---------------|
68: ```
69: 
70: For each MUST_READ paper, also show:
71: - **Why relevant**: Which LLM keywords triggered the high score
72: - **First author**: Name + institution (if detected) + S2 profile (if checked)
73: - **Abstract**: Full abstract for quick assessment
74: 
75: #### INTERESTING (score 2–3.9)
76: 
77: Same table format, but only show first 2 lines of abstract.
78: 
79: #### MARGINAL (score 0.5–1.9)
80: 
81: Compact table only — title and score. No abstract.
82: 
83: ### Step 4: Author Quality Assessment
84: 
85: When `check-authors` is enabled (or for MUST_READ papers by default):
86: 
87: 1. **First author OpenAlex profile**: h-index, citation count, works count
88: 2. **Institution detection**: Match against known institution lists
89:    - **Tier 1**: OpenAI, DeepMind, Anthropic, Meta FAIR, Google Research/Brain, MS Research, top US/UK/CN universities
90:    - **Tier 2**: Strong regional universities, major tech companies (Alibaba, ByteDance, Tencent, Baidu, Huawei)
91:    - **Unknown**: No match found
92: 
93: 3. **Author quality labels**:
94:    - `high` — h-index ≥ 20 OR citations ≥ 5000 (established researcher)
95:    - `medium` — h-index ≥ 10 OR citations ≥ 1000 (productive researcher)
96:    - `low` — citations ≥ 100 but no strong track record (early career or potentially water papers)
97:    - `unknown` — OpenAlex lookup failed or no profile found
98: 
99: 4. **Water paper signals** (flag, don't auto-exclude):
100:    - First author with very low citation count AND from unknown institution
101:    - Title contains "survey" or "review" from unknown group
102:    - Paper is solely about applying existing method to a new domain without innovation
103:    - Multiple papers from same group with near-identical titles in short timeframe
104: 
105: ### Step 5: Save Summary
106: 
107: Save a dated summary to `knowledge/daily/`:
108: 
109: ```
110: knowledge/daily/YYYY-MM-DD.md
111: ```
112: 
113: Format:
114: 
115: ```markdown
116: # Daily LLM Papers — YYYY-MM-DD
117: 
118: ## MUST_READ (N)
119: 
120: ### [Title](arxiv_url)
121: - **Authors**: First Author et al. | **Institution**: [detected or Unknown] | **Quality**: [high/medium/low]
122: - **Score**: X.X | **Why**: [key LLM keywords that matched]
123: - **Abstract**: [full abstract]
124: 
125: ## INTERESTING (N)
126: 
127: | Paper | Score | Authors | Note |
128: |-------|-------|---------|------|
129: 
130: ## SKIPPED (N non-LLM papers filtered out)
131: 
132: Top skipped: [list 3-5 titles that were close to threshold]
133: ```
134: 
135: ### Step 6: Suggest Follow-up
136: 
137: For MUST_READ papers:
138: 
139: ```text
140: /arxiv "ARXIV_ID" - download    — download LaTeX source and read in detail
141: /research-lit "topic"            — broader literature context
142: ```
143: 
144: ## Relevance Scoring Logic
145: 
146: The scoring system uses weighted keyword matching:
147: 
148: **Positive signals (LLM relevance)**:
149: - **+2**: Core LLM keywords — "large language model", "LLM", "RLHF", "DPO", "chain-of-thought", "scaling law", "fine-tuning", specific model names (GPT-4, LLaMA, DeepSeek, etc.)
150: - **+1**: LLM-adjacent — "transformer", "MoE", "quantization", "RAG", "agent", "multimodal", "safety", "alignment", "KV cache", "language model"
151: - **+0.5**: Tangential — "NLP", "pretrain", "benchmark", "evaluation"
152: 
153: **Negative signals (non-LLM exclusion)**:
154: - **-3**: Strong exclude — "object detection", "video synthesis", "speech recognition", "medical imaging", "protein", "autonomous driving", "recommender system", "GAN"
155: - **-1**: Mild exclude — "diffusion model" (unless paired with LLM keywords), "image generation", "video generation"
156: 
157: **Tier thresholds**:
158: - `MUST_READ` ≥ 4: Directly about LLM methodology, training, or capabilities
159: - `INTERESTING` ≥ 2: LLM-related but may be application-focused or peripheral
160: - `MARGINAL` ≥ 0.5: Tangentially related, probably not core LLM work
161: - `SKIP` < 0.5: Not LLM-related
162: 
163: ## Key Rules
164: 
165: - **PDF IS BANNED.** Never suggest downloading PDFs. Only LaTeX source.
166: - Only surface LLM papers. If in doubt, exclude rather than include.
167: - Author quality is advisory, not deterministic — a paper from an unknown author
168:   can still be MUST_READ if the content is clearly important.
169: - Water paper flags are signals, not verdicts. Present them and let the user decide.
170: - Always show WHY a paper was scored the way it was (which keywords matched).
171: - For MUST_READ papers from top institutions, highlight the institution.
172: - The scoring system is conservative: better to miss a borderline paper than
173:   to flood the user with irrelevant work.
174: - If the arXiv API is unreachable, suggest checking https://huggingface.co/papers
175:   or https://x.com/akaboratory as fallback.
